 {"id":"bd-2sh","title":"Phase 10: Documentation and User Guides","description":"## Purpose
Create comprehensive documentation for the console integration, covering API reference, user guides, agent compatibility notes, and integration patterns.

## Background
Good documentation ensures:
- Users can quickly get started
- Advanced features are discoverable
- Agent authors understand compatibility
- Contributors understand the design

## Key Deliverables

### 1. API Documentation
- Rustdoc for all public types and traits
- Example code in doc comments
- Links between related items
- Feature flag documentation

### 2. Console User Guide
- Quick start (5-minute setup)
- Output mode configuration
- Theme customization
- Renderable catalog with examples

### 3. Agent Compatibility Guide
- How agent detection works
- Environment variables reference
- Stream separation semantics
- Writing agent-compatible code

### 4. Integration Patterns
- Attaching console to sessions
- Global vs explicit console
- Custom renderable creation
- Performance considerations

### 5. README Updates
- Update main sqlmodel README
- Add console feature section
- Include screenshots of output
- Link to detailed docs

## Documentation Locations
- crates/sqlmodel-console/README.md - crate overview
- docs/console/ - detailed guides
- Rustdoc in source files - API reference
- examples/ - runnable examples with comments

## Writing Standards
- Concise, action-oriented
- Code examples for everything
- Both rich and plain output shown
- Agent considerations always noted

## Dependencies
- All console features complete
- Visual examples finalized
- API stable

## Verification
- cargo doc builds without warnings
- All public items documented
- Examples in docs compile
- User guide tested by newcomer","acceptance_criteria":"API documentation covers all public types and functions

 {"id":"bd-2ux","title":"Implement Session.refresh()","description":"## Description

Immediately reload object from database.

## Python Behavior

```python
# Reload all attributes
session.refresh(user)

# Reload specific attributes
session.refresh(user, ['name', 'email'])

# With options (eager load relationships)
session.refresh(user, with_for_update=True)
```

Unlike expire(), refresh() immediately queries the DB.

## Rust Implementation

```rust
impl Session {
    pub async fn refresh<M: Model>(
        &mut self,
        cx: &Cx,
        model: &mut M,
        attributes: Option<&[&str]>,
    ) -> Outcome<(), Error> {
        let pk = model.primary_key_value();
        
        // Query database
        let fresh: M = select!(M)
            .filter(M::primary_key().eq(pk))
            .one(cx, &self.conn)
            .await?;
        
        // Update model
        match attributes {
            Some(attrs) => model.copy_attributes_from(&fresh, attrs),
            None => *model = fresh,
        }
        
        // Update identity map
        self.identity_map.update(model);
        
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Immediately queries database
- [ ] Updates model with fresh values
- [ ] Can refresh specific attributes only
- [ ] Updates identity map
- [ ] Errors if object not in DB (was deleted)

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/session/refresh.rs)
- [ ] Test refresh reloads from DB
- [ ] Test refresh with attribute subset
- [ ] Test refresh clears pending changes
- [ ] Test refresh updates identity map
- [ ] Test refresh with relationships

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/session_refresh.rs)
- [ ] Modify externally → refresh → sees new values
- [ ] refresh specific attributes only
- [ ] refresh with eager-loaded relationships
- [ ] refresh after expire
- [ ] refresh detached object fails

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Refresh query issued
- [ ] TRACE: Fields refreshed

 {"id":"bd-2x6","title":"Implement passive_deletes for relationships","description":"## Description

Support passive_deletes to let database handle cascade deletes.

## Python Behavior

```python
class Parent(SQLModel, table=True):
    id: int
    children: List['Child'] = Relationship(
        back_populates='parent',
        passive_deletes=True  # Let DB handle deletes via FK ON DELETE
    )
    
    # With 'all', don't even warn about orphans
    children2: List['Child'] = Relationship(passive_deletes='all')
```

When passive_deletes=True:
- SQLAlchemy doesn't emit DELETE for children
- Relies on DB's ON DELETE CASCADE
- More efficient for large collections

## Rust Implementation

```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct Parent {
    id: i32,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship, passive_deletes)]
    children: Related<Vec<Child>>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship, passive_deletes = "all")]
    children2: Related<Vec<Child>>,
}
```

## Behavior

- passive_deletes=false (default): ORM deletes related objects
- passive_deletes=true: ORM relies on DB cascade
- passive_deletes='all': Don't even track orphans

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] passive_deletes attribute parsed
- [ ] Session.delete() respects passive_deletes
- [ ] Works with ON DELETE CASCADE FK constraint
- [ ] 'all' variant prevents orphan tracking

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/relationship.rs)
- [ ] Test passive_deletes=True parsed
- [ ] Test passive_deletes="all" parsed
- [ ] Test affects ON DELETE behavior
- [ ] Test with different FK constraints

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/passive_deletes.rs)
- [ ] Delete parent → DB cascades children (passive)
- [ ] passive_deletes="all" for all operations
- [ ] passive_deletes + ON DELETE CASCADE
- [ ] No orphan cleanup with passive
- [ ] Compare active vs passive delete behavior

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: passive_deletes configuration
- [ ] TRACE: Delete cascade behavior

-{"id":"bd-2ybr","title":"Implement built-in validators (uuid, ipv4, ipv6, mac_address)","description":"## Description

Add common validation patterns as built-in validators.

## Python Validators

```python
from pydantic import Field

class Network(SQLModel):
    device_id: str = Field(pattern=r'^[0-9a-f]{8}-...$')  # UUID pattern
    ipv4: str  # IPv4Address type does validation
    ipv6: str  # IPv6Address type does validation  
    mac: str = Field(pattern=r'^([0-9A-Fa-f]{2}:){5}[0-9A-Fa-f]{2}$')
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
#[derive(Model, Validate)]
struct Network {
    #[validate(uuid)]
    device_id: String,
    
    #[validate(ipv4)]
    ipv4: String,
    
    #[validate(ipv6)]
    ipv6: String,
    
    #[validate(mac_address)]
    mac: String,
    
    #[validate(slug)]
    url_slug: String,
    
    #[validate(credit_card)]
    card_number: String,
}
```

## Built-in Patterns

- uuid: UUID format
- ipv4: IPv4 address
- ipv6: IPv6 address
- mac_address: MAC address
- slug: URL-safe slug
- credit_card: Credit card number (Luhn check)
- phone: Phone number (E.164)
- hex_color: Hex color code

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] uuid validator
- [ ] ipv4 validator
- [ ] ipv6 validator
- [ ] mac_address validator
- [ ] slug validator
- [ ] credit_card validator (Luhn)
- [ ] Clear error messages for each

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/validate.rs)
- [ ] Test uuid validator accepts valid UUID
- [ ] Test uuid validator rejects invalid
- [ ] Test ipv4 validator
- [ ] Test ipv6 validator
- [ ] Test mac_address validator
- [ ] Test custom format validators

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/builtin_validators.rs)
- [ ] Field with uuid validation
- [ ] Field with ipv4 validation
- [ ] Field with ipv6 validation
- [ ] Field with mac_address validation
- [ ] Combined validators on one field
- [ ] Custom format registration

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Validator pattern matching
- [ ] TRACE: Validation failure details

 {"id":"bd-2z7a","title":"Implement dynamic model creation","description":"## Description

Create models dynamically at runtime.

## Python Behavior

```python
from sqlmodel import SQLModel, Field
from typing import Optional

def create_model(name: str, fields: dict):
    return type(name, (SQLModel,), {
        '__annotations__': fields,
        '__tablename__': name.lower(),
    })

DynamicUser = create_model('DynamicUser', {
    'id': Optional[int],
    'name': str,
})
```

## Rust Approach

Rust's static typing makes true dynamic models impossible, but we can:

### Option 1: Generic Dynamic Model
```rust
pub struct DynamicModel {
    table_name: String,
    columns: HashMap<String, ColumnDef>,
    values: HashMap<String, Value>,
}

impl DynamicModel {
    pub fn new(table_name: &str) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn add_column(&mut self, name: &str, def: ColumnDef) { ... }
    pub fn set(&mut self, name: &str, value: Value) { ... }
    pub fn get(&self, name: &str) -> Option<&Value> { ... }
}
```

### Option 2: Schema from database
```rust
let schema = introspect_table(cx, conn, "users").await?;
let dynamic = DynamicModel::from_schema(schema);
```

## Use Cases

- Database introspection tools
- Generic admin interfaces
- Schema migration tools

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] DynamicModel type
- [ ] Runtime column definition
- [ ] Query execution with dynamic models
- [ ] Serialization to/from JSON
- [ ] Table creation from dynamic model

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/dynamic.rs)
- [ ] Test create_model function
- [ ] Test field definitions at runtime
- [ ] Test validators on dynamic models
- [ ] Test inheritance for dynamic models

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/dynamic_models.rs)
- [ ] create_model("User", [("name", str)]) works
- [ ] Dynamic model validates correctly
- [ ] Dynamic model serializes correctly
- [ ] Dynamic model in database operations
- [ ] Performance vs static models

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Dynamic model creation
- [ ] TRACE: Field definition processing

 {"id":"bd-2zf","title":"Implement IndeterminateSpinner for unknown-length operations","description":"## Purpose
Create a spinner renderable for operations where we do not know the total count or duration, showing activity with elapsed time.

## Background
Many database operations have unknown duration:
- Establishing connections
- Running complex queries
- Waiting for locks
- Initial data discovery

Users need feedback that something is happening, even without a completion percentage.

## Implementation Details

### File Location
crates/sqlmodel-console/src/renderables/spinner.rs

### Core Struct
IndeterminateSpinner holds:
- message: String
- started_at: Instant
- style: SpinnerStyle enum
- theme: Theme

### SpinnerStyle Enum
- Dots: ... (three dots cycling)
- Braille: Unicode braille pattern animation
- Line: -/|\ rotating
- Arrow: arrow rotating
- Simple: asterisk blinking

### Frame Generation
Each style defines frames array and interval_ms.
Example Dots: [".", "..", "...", ".."] at 250ms intervals

### Rendering (Rich Mode)
[...] Connecting to database (2.3s)
^---- spinner animation
     ^---- message
                           ^---- elapsed time

### Plain Text (Agent Mode)
[...] Connecting to database (2.3s)
(Static output, no animation - agents see single line)

## API Design
IndeterminateSpinner::new(message)
  .style(SpinnerStyle::Dots)
  .theme(theme)
  .render(width) / .render_plain()

### Convert to Progress
When length becomes known, convert spinner to progress:
let progress = spinner.into_progress(total);

### Live Update Pattern
let spinner = IndeterminateSpinner::new("Connecting");
console.start_spinner(&spinner);
// ... do work
console.stop_spinner(&spinner);

## Verification Steps
1. Test each spinner style
2. Verify elapsed time formatting (s, m:ss)
3. Test conversion to progress bar
4. Verify plain text output
5. Test with long messages (truncation)
6. Verify animation timing

## Dependencies
- Theme from this crate
- std::time::Instant for timing","acceptance_criteria":"IndeterminateSpinner shows activity animation
Spinner has multiple style options
Spinner respects terminal width
Plain mode outputs dots or status text
All unit tests verify animation frames
Performance tests confirm low overhead","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:12:38.251317294Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-21T11:25:34.436765300Z","closed_at":"2026-01-21T11:25:34.436722440Z","close_reason":"Implemented IndeterminateSpinner with 5 styles, conversion to progress bar, and 20 unit tests","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2zf","depends_on_id":"bd-1q2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:53:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-2zf","depends_on_id":"bd-1rn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:53:31Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":17,"issue_id":"bd-2zf","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Unit Tests to Implement

1. test_spinner_creation - verify initial state
2. test_spinner_all_styles - verify each SpinnerStyle
3. test_spinner_frame_generation - verify animation frames
4. test_spinner_elapsed_time - verify duration tracking
5. test_spinner_render_plain - verify text output
6. test_spinner_message_update - verify message changes
7. test_spinner_convert_to_progress - verify conversion
8. test_spinner_render_rich - verify rich output (with feature)","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:27:07Z"}]}
